planting.note: Sow seeds in fall (cold stratification over winter triggers spring germination) or cold-stratify for 4–6 weeks before indoor sowing in late winter; oxlip grows best in moist, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in part shade. It naturalizes in woodland and meadow settings and was historically associated with specific ancient woodland indicator communities in England. All parts are toxic if ingested in quantity.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in part to full shade; the oxlip is one of the three native British primroses and occupies an interesting ecological and horticultural position between the two more familiar species — it is rare in the wild (restricted in Britain to a few hundred ancient woodland sites in East Anglia), yet it has the pendant, one-sided cluster of pale-yellow flowers on a single stem characteristic of the cowslip combined with the broader, primrose-like leaves and woodland habitat of the primrose. Where cowslip and primrose both grow, the oxlip hybrid (Primula x variabilis, better known as the polyanthus) arises naturally. The true oxlip has pale, almost-cream-yellow pendant flowers in one-sided clusters on stems 6–12 inches tall, appearing in April and May. In cultivation it requires the woodland conditions of its native habitat — moist, shaded, slightly alkaline soil — and is somewhat less easily pleased than cowslip or primrose. A collector's plant for the serious woodland garden, valuable for the historical significance of its conservation ecology and the delicate beauty of its pale spring flowers.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes horizontally 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in fall in shade; yellow wood anemone goes dormant by early summer — mark the planting site to avoid disturbing the rhizomes. It spreads slowly into expanding colonies. All parts are toxic if ingested and the sap may cause skin irritation.

directions: Plant rhizomes in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to part shade; yellow wood anemone is a European counterpart to the familiar white wood anemone (Anemone nemorosa), producing the same low, spreading, rhizomatous mats of deeply divided, three-parted leaves in early spring and similarly ephemeral flowers — but in clear, warm golden-yellow rather than white, each bloom a simple, cup-shaped, buttercup-like star of 5–8 petals that appears singly on 4–8 inch stems in March through April before the forest canopy closes. A true spring ephemeral — the entire plant emerges, flowers, sets seed, and dies back to the underground rhizome within 8–10 weeks, completely disappearing by early summer. The warm yellow color, contrasting with the cool whites and blues of woodland wildflower companions (wood anemone, bluebells, grape hyacinth), adds a welcome warmth to the spring woodland tapestry. Both species hybridize naturally where they meet, producing the hybrid Anemone x lipsiensis with pale primrose-yellow flowers. Outstanding for massing under deciduous trees in naturalistic woodland gardens.

planting.note: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in fall in shade; California fawn lily requires a cool, moist growing season (spring) and summer dormancy — do not water or disturb after the foliage dies back in summer. It takes 3–5 years from a corm to reach blooming size and may take 2 years to establish after transplanting. Do not allow corms to dry out between purchase and planting.

directions: Plant corms in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full to part shade; California fawn lily is a beautiful native wildflower of the Pacific Coast ranges and Sierra Nevada foothills, producing two broad, glossy, mottled leaves (the mottling in dark and light green gives the appearance of a fawn's spots — hence 'fawn lily') and in March through May one to three reflexed, lily-like flowers of pure white to cream-white with yellow centers and gracefully swept-back petals that curve fully back like a Turk's cap, each nodding on an arching stem above the paired mottled leaves. The combination of the beautifully mottled foliage and the elegant, reflexed white flowers creates a plant of extraordinary refinement in the woodland garden. Like all erythroniums it is a spring ephemeral — disappearing completely by early summer. The cultivar 'White Beauty' is a vigorous, large-flowered selection widely available and one of the finest white flowers of the spring shade garden. It forms offsets slowly to create small but beautiful colonies over years.

planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring or divide clumps in spring or early fall in well-drained, moderately fertile to lean soil in full sun to part shade; pyramid bugle does not produce stolons and forms neat, non-invasive clumps — it is the best choice where the running nature of Ajuga reptans would cause problems in beds or rocky crevices. The cultivar 'Metallica Crispa' has unusual, crinkled, metallic-brown to bronze-purple foliage.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile to lean soil in full sun to part shade; pyramid bugle is a compact, clump-forming European species closely related to the familiar carpet bugleweed (Ajuga reptans) but distinguished by forming non-running clumps rather than the spreading stolons that make A. reptans useful as a groundcover but invasive in gardens. Pyramid bugle is named for its unusually bold, densely pyramidal flower spike — in spring, each clump sends up one to several thick, densely leaved, upright spikes with large, overlapping bracts and rich violet-blue tubular flowers packed tightly in whorls, creating a fuller, more architectural flower spike than the trailing stems of A. reptans. The decorative bracts on the flower spike are larger and more prominent than in the common bugleweed. The cultivar 'Metallica Crispa' is especially ornamental — its leaves are small, strongly crinkled and puckered in texture, and have a remarkable metallic, bronze-purple to chocolate-brown sheen throughout the season. An excellent choice for rock gardens, wall crevices, and edges where a neat, contained bugle that won't run is desired.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to part shade; intermediate periwinkle is less cold-hardy than V. minor (hardy to zone 4) or V. major (hardy to zone 6), surviving reliably in zones 7–9. It blooms in late autumn and winter when temperatures are mild, providing off-season color that the other periwinkles do not offer. It may naturalize in mild climates.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to part shade; intermediate periwinkle is a less well-known Mediterranean species that occupies a horticultural niche distinct from both of the commonly grown periwinkles (V. minor and V. major) in one key way — it blooms in autumn and winter rather than spring. From October through February in zones 8–10, it produces the characteristic five-petaled, pinwheel periwinkle flowers in pale, milky-white to palest lavender-white or soft blue-lavender, adding off-season color to the shade garden when almost nothing else is in bloom. The evergreen to semi-evergreen leaves are narrower and glossier than those of V. major, giving the plant a slightly finer texture. It spreads by trailing, rooting stems to form a weed-suppressing groundcover in dry shade under trees — notably more drought-tolerant than V. minor in warm climates. The cultivar 'Wojo's Gem' has cream-variegated leaves with white margins and is particularly ornamental. A useful addition to the winter-interest shade garden in Gulf Coast and Mediterranean-climate gardens where mild winters allow its distinctive flowering season to be fully appreciated.
